content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

November 28, 1990

Steven F. Nord
City Attorney
City of Merced
678 West 18th Street
Merced, CA 95340

Re: Your Request for Confirmation of
Telephone Advice
Our File No. A-90-674

Dear Mr. Nord:

We have received your letter dated October 22, 1990, seeking
written confirmation of the telephone advice I provided on October
13, 1990 regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act ("the Act").1

Your letter accurately summarizes the advice that I provided
to you. As we discussed during our telephone conversation, you
represent Bruce Gabriault, Mayor of Merced. Mayor Gabriault owns
a television/appliance business and the real property where the
business operates. The property is approximately 1700 feet from a
proposed civic center/hotel project. According to you, only
approximately 15% of the city's businesses are located within a
2500 foot radius of the proposed project.

You stated in our telephone conversation that Mayor
Gabriault's interest in the real property is more than $1,000.
You also stated that the council's decision to go forward with the
project would foreseeably increase the fair market value of the
Mayor Gabriault's property by $10,000 or more. I advised you that
under these facts, Mayor Gabriault has a conflict of interest if
he makes, participates in making, or in any way uses his official
position to influence a decision to go forward with the project.
(Section 87100, 87103; Regulation 18702.3(a)(3), copy enclosed.)

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-90-674
Page 2


     Under Section 87100 Mayor Gabriault may not make, participate
in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a decision to go forward with the project if he knows or
has reason to know he has a financial interest in the decision.
Mayor Gabriault will have a "financial interest" in the decision
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the
public generally, on "[a]ny property in which [he] has a
director of indirect interest worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or
more." (Section 87103(b).)

     As we discussed on the telephone, it is reasonably foresee-
able that the decision to go forward with the project will
increase the fair market value of the Mayor's property by $10,000
or more, and therefore will have a material financial effect on
his interest. (Regulation 18702.3(a)(3).)

     You also inquired whether the fact that 15 percent of the
city's businesses are located within a 2500 foot radius would
cause application of the "public generally" exception of Section
87103 (copy enclosed). As you know, in order for the conflict-of-interest prohibitions of the Act to apply, the
effect of the governmental decision on the public official must be
distinguishable from the decision's effect upon the public
generally. (Section 18703; Regulation 18703.) That 15 percent of
the city's businesses are located within a 2500 foot radius would
not cause the "public generally" exception to apply. (See
Hentschke Advice Letter, No. A-90-520, copy enclosed.)

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter
please contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [signature]

                                   By:  Mark T. Morodomi
                                        Counsel, Legal Division

SH:MTM:dg
